policy_statement: Require regulated utilities and critical infrastructure operators to incorporate updated climate projections into required vulnerability assessments as updated and periodically review resilience standards for incorporation into PSC-approved risk-reduction plans; support non-PSC utilities in developing their own requirements/plans (PSC).
mechanism_description: The PSC should require utilities and critical infrastructure operators to use updated climate projections in vulnerability assessments and to periodically review resilience standards so the findings are incorporated into risk-reduction plans, improving infrastructure resilience to climate hazards.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard projections + infrastructure planning/standards → ↓ exposure & ↓ sensitivity (↑ robustness/redundancy) → ↓ damage/disruption from extreme events/long-term climate shifts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system/human activity: Utilities and critical infrastructure operators must perform vulnerability assessments using updated climate projections and periodically update resilience standards, integrating findings into regulator-approved risk-reduction plans.  
(2) Mediating physical/process link: Better hazard characterization and set design/operational standards drive upgrades (e.g., hardening, elevation, redundancy, flood protection, heat tolerance) and improved maintenance/operations aligned with future conditions.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced likelihood and severity of service outages and asset damage under climate-driven hazards (flooding, storm surge, extreme heat, wildfire, etc.), improving resilience and reducing societal/economic losses.
dominant_pathway_test: Requiring climate-projection-based vulnerability assessments and resilience standards most strongly reduces climate-hazard impacts on critical infrastructure by lowering exposure/sensitivity and improving preparedness.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Climate hazard information is translated into required assessments and enforceable resilience standards, leading to infrastructure hardening/operational changes that reduce hazard impacts—an adaptation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While some measures may incidentally reduce emissions (e.g., fewer emergency generators, less rebuilding) or improve efficiency, the intended and largest effect is reduced vulnerability and improved continuity under climate hazards.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a jurisdiction framed the same tool primarily to cut GHGs (e.g., climate-risk disclosure used mainly to drive decarbonization investment), categorization could differ, but that is not the dominant outcome of this mechanism as defined (resilience standards/risk-reduction).  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that mandates climate-projection-based assessments and periodic resilience standard updates aims to reduce infrastructure vulnerability to climate impacts; thus Adaptation is consistently primary across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype note: Any finer-grained subtype (e.g., coastal flooding vs. heat resilience; electricity vs. water systems) should be assigned at the individual policy level based on explicit hazard/asset details, and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could have minor mitigation co-benefits (avoided reconstruction emissions, reduced outage-related generator use), but these are typically secondary; classification would only change if the mechanism were explicitly designed primarily to drive emissions reductions rather than resilience.
key_indicators: “PSC should require”; “incorporate updated climate change projections”; “required vulnerability assessments”; “periodically review established resilience standards”; “incorporation into PSC-approved risk-reduction plans”; “supported in the development of their own requirements and plans”
co_benefits: Improved general reliability and emergency preparedness; reduced non-climate outage risks; potential public safety and economic continuity benefits.
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes an enabling component (“should be supported”) for non-PSC utilities, but the dominant instrument is the PSC’s mandatory requirement for regulated entities; could also be framed as a planning/strategy requirement, but the regulatory compulsion is primary.
